title: Lithium Alkylselenolates and -tellurolates − A Solid-State and Solution Structural Study
abstract:
SHORT COMMUNICATION
Lithium Alkylselenolates and -tellurolates 2 A Solid-State and Solution
Structural Study
William Clegg,[a] Robert P. Davies,*[b] Ronald Snaith,[c][†] and Andrew E. H. Wheatley[c]
Keywords: Chalcogens / Lithium / Insertion / NMR spectroscopy
Facile insertion of elemental Se or Te into the C−Li bond of
nBuLi in the presence of TMEDA gives [nBuXLi·TMEDA]2
(X = Se 1; Te 2). Both 1 and 2 have been fully characterised
in the solid and solution states resulting in the first crystal
Introduction
The desire for new and efficient syntheses for both metal
chalcogenolates (primarily used as precursors to semicon-
ducting metal chalcogenolides[1] and as model systems for
various biologically active metalloproteins[2]) and also for
new reagents for the synthesis of selenium- and tellurium-
containing organics[3] has resulted in a growing interest in
alkali metal complexes with selenolate (RSe2) and tellurol-
ate (RTe2) ligands. However, there still remains a scarcity
of both solid-state and solution studies of these species, es-
pecially when compared to the multitude of data available
on the analogous alkali metal alkoxide (RO2) and thiolate
(RS2) complexes.[4] Moreover, the majority of studies to
date have utilised sterically bulky ligands, such as substi-
tuted silyl chalcogenolates (R3SiX2; R 5 Me, SiMe3; X 5
Se, Te)[529] or substituted aromatics (Mes*X2; Mes* 5
2,4,6-tBu3C6H2; X 5 Se, Te)[10212] so as to confer extra
kinetic stability upon the resulting complex. To the best of
our knowledge the only other examples of alkali-metal or-
ganochalcogenolates to be structurally characterised are the
2,29-bipyridine
complexes
[PhSeLi·bipy]2
and
[2-Pyr-
SeLi·bipy]2,[13]
the
phenylacetyl
selenolate
PhC;CSe-
Li·TMEDA·THF[14]
and
the
heterobimetallic
species
[CpFe{C5H3(CH2NMe2)TeLi·DME}],[15]
[(C5H5)2Lu(µ-
SePh)2Li·2THF][16] and [(py)2Yb(SePh)2(µ-SePh)2Li(py)2]
(py 5 pyridine).[17] In order to confront this lack of struc-
tural knowledge, particularly of the less sterically de-
manding ligands, we report here the first solid-state charac-
terisation
of
both
a
lithium
alkylselenolate
(nBuSeLi·TMEDA)2
(1)
and
-tellurolate
(nBuTeLi·
TMEDA)2 (2) [TMEDA 5 (Me2NCH2)2].
[a] Dept. of Chemistry, Bedson Building, University of Newcastle
upon Tyne,
Newcastle upon Tyne NE1 7RU, UK
[b] Dept. of Chemistry, Imperial College of Science, Technology
and Medicine,
Exhibition Road, London SW7 2AY, UK
Email: r.davies@ic.ac.uk
[c] Department of Chemistry, University of Cambridge,
Lensfield Road, Cambridge CB2 1EW, UK
[†] Deceased.
Eur. J. Inorg. Chem. 2001, 141121413
WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, 2001
143421948/01/060621411 $ 17.501.50/0
1411
structures of a lithium alkylselenolate and -tellurolate and
giving a unique insight into the coordination chemistry of the
less sterically demanding chalcogenolate ligands.
Results and Discussion
Two main synthetic routes have been documented for the
preparation of lithium chalcogenolates: metallation of an
organic acid (RSeH or RTeH) and insertion of elemental
Se or Te into a C2Li or Si2Li bond.[2,3] The second of
these procedures was employed in the synthesis of 1 and 2
since it provided a clean, high yield of the desired products
and avoided the synthesis of the readily oxidised butylse-
lenol and butyltellurol species. Reaction in toluene of nBuLi
with elemental Se or Te (1:1 ratio) in the presence of
TMEDA (1 equivalent) resulted in the dissolution of the
Group 16 element to give a clear pale yellow solution from
which it proved possible to grow, at room temperature, crys-
tals of the lithium chalcogenolates 1 and 2 in yields 36 and
52%, respectively. Both 1 and 2 are highly air sensitive and
extremely pungent compounds.
A low temperature X-ray structural characterisation of 1
and 2 reveals that they both crystallise in the monoclinic
space group I2/a and that they both contain two isostructu-
ral dimeric molecules within the asymmetric unit, each of
which exhibits twofold symmetry (Figure 1). These dimeric
units are based around a central Li2X2 (X 5 Se, Te) ring
with the n-butyl groups in a cis conformation and each of
the lithium ions solvated by one TMEDA molecule. The
free ends of the butyl groups are disordered in the solid-
state structures of both 1 and 2. The Se2Li mean average
bond length in 1 [2.583 A˚ ; range 2.544(17) to 2.592(15) A˚ ]
is comparable to the Se2Li bond lengths of previously
studied lithium selenolates [range 2.462(13) to 2.622(8)
A˚ ].[5,11214] However, the Se2C bond lengths in 1 [1.98(1)
and 2.02(2) A˚ ] are longer than the Se2C bonds in the previ-
ously
characterised
arylselenolates
[range
1.896(4)
to
1.942(7) A˚ ][11213] and also longer than the Se-C bond
[1.813(4)
A˚ ]
in
the
alkylnylselenolate
[PhC;CSe-
Li·TMEDA·THF].[14] This can be accounted for by the sp3
hybridisation of the α-carbon in 1 whose larger, more dif-
fuse orbitals form longer bonds than the α-carbon sp2 hy-
brid orbitals in the arylselenolates and the α-carbon sp or-
bitals in the alkynylselenolate. The C2Se2Li angle [mean
95.8°, range 92.9(5) to 97.4(7)°] is more acute than in any
 W. Clegg, R. P. Davies, R. Snaith, A. E. H. Wheatley
SHORT COMMUNICATION
other selenolate previously reported [range 98.2(3) to
104.3(2)°][11214] presumably by virtue of the low steric de-
mands of the n-butyl group. The Li2Se2Li inter-ring angle
is also very acute, 69.2(6) and 70.1(6)° in 1 cf. 77.0(2) and
75.8(4)° in the only other dimeric lithium selenolates [Li-
(DME)SeSi(SiMe3)3]2 [5] and [Li(bipy)(SePh)]2,[13] respect-
ively.
Figure 1. The solid-state structure of [nBuSeLi·TMEDA]2 (1); only
one of the two isostructural dimers in the asymmetric unit is
shown; hydrogen atoms and disorder in the n-butyl group are omit-
ted for clarity; the molecule lies on a crystallographic twofold rota-
tion axis which relates Li1 and Se1 to Li1A and Se2A
In 2 the Te2Li distances [mean 2.813 A˚ , range 2.802(6)
to 2.824(6) A˚ ] are similar to previously reported Li2Te dis-
tances [range 2.70(1) to 2.88(2) A˚ ][6210,15] and the Te2C
(sp3) distances of 2.259(11) and 2.191(4) A˚ are larger than
the
Te2C(sp2)
distances
of
2.142(2)
A˚
in
[(o-
C6H4CH2NMe2)TeLi(DME)], 2.184(4) A˚
in [Mes*TeLi-
(THF)3][10]
and
2.122(2)
A˚
in
[CpFe{C5H3-
(CH2NMe2)TeLi·DME}].[15] The Li2Te2Li bond angles
[both of which are 68.2(2) °] are more acute than those ob-
served [75.1(1) °] in the only other dimeric lithium tellurol-
ate [(DME)LiTeSi(SiMe3)3]2,[9] and the mean C2Te2Li
bond angle in 2 is 95.0° [range 93.5(2) to 97.2(2)°].
In addition to solid-state X-ray diffraction studies, the
solution structures of both 1 and 2 were probed by multinu-
clear NMR spectroscopy and crysocopic relative molecular
mass measurements. Cryoscopic measurements on a 0.030
solution of 1 in benzene indicated an average association
value n for [BuSeLi·TMEDA]n of 3.17 6 0.08. One possible
explanation of this association value is that in nondonor
solvents a dimer/tetramer equilibrium is occurring, in which
the tetramer is formed from the back-to-back stacking of
two of the cis dimeric units to give a Li4Se4 cubane struc-
ture. A similar result was obtained with a 0.030 solution
of 2 (n 5 3.30 6 0.12). Despite this, 7Li NMR spectroscopic
studies at 25 °C on THF solutions of 1 and 2 showed only
one peak (δ 5 20.04 for a 0.023 solution of 1, and δ 5
0.25 for a 0.020 solution of 2, referenced to PhLi in THF)
indicating that any fluxional behaviour between different
aggregation states is rapid. This dynamic equilibrium was
still observed down to temperatures as low as 280 °C since
even at these temperatures only one peak was visible by
Eur. J. Inorg. Chem. 2001, 141121413
1412
7Li NMR spectroscopy. The absence of any observable 7Li
coupling to either 77Se or 125Te in these spectra is also indic-
ative of the fluxional behaviour of these species, since any
change in aggregation state would involve the breaking and
formation of Li2X (X 5 Se or Te) bonds. As expected, the
1H and 13C NMR spectra of 1 and 2 in [D6]DMSO showed
peaks attributable to the TMEDA and n-butyl groups, while
the main difference between the two 13C NMR spectra was
the position of the α-carbon atoms: δ 5 21.1 in 1 and δ 5
219.4 in 2. In addition, 1JCTe coupling was observed in the
13C spectrum of 2, with a coupling constant of 158.0 Hz
(this is comparable to previously reported values for dialkyl
tellurides).[18] It proved impossible to see Se2C coupling in
the 13C NMR spectrum of 1 due to the ready decomposi-
tion of this product, hence the poor resolution of its spec-
tra. However, 77Se{1H} NMR spectroscopy of 1 was under-
taken and showed a single peak at δ 5 2660 (referenced to
SeMe2 at δ 5 0); again no Se2Li coupling was observable.
We now hope to extend this work to look at other
sterically undemanding lithium chalcogenolates and also
their transmetallation reactions, with particular emphasis
placed upon their possible utility in the synthesis of single
source precursors to semiconducting materials.[1]
Experimental Section
All reactions and manipulations were carried out under an inert
atmosphere of dry nitrogen using standard double manifold and
glove-box techniques.
[nBuSeLi·TMEDA]2 (1): nBuLi (3.2 mL, 1.6 in hexanes,
5.0 mmol) was added to a suspension of Se (0.40 g, 5 mmol) in
toluene (10 mL) and TMEDA (0.75 mL, 5 mmol) under nitrogen
at 278 °C. The mixture was then allowed to warm to room temper-
ature with constant stirring to give a pale yellow solution. This
solution was filtered under nitrogen and reduced in volume under
vacuum to approximately 3 mL. Hexane (6 mL) was then added
and the solution left at room temperature for 24 hours to yield a
batch of yellow crystalline needles (0.46 g, 36%). 2 M.p. 1282130
°C. 2 1H NMR (400 MHz, C6D6): δ 5 2.64 (t, 2 H, -CH2Se-), 2.23
(s, 12 H, TMEDA), 2.13 (s, 4 H, TMEDA), 1.95 (m, 2 H, -CH2-),
1.64 (m, 2 H, -CH2-), 1.11 (t, 3 H, CH3CH2-).
[nBuTeLi·TMEDA]2 (2): nBuLi (3.2 mL, 1.6 in hexanes,
5.0 mmol) was added to a suspension of Te (0.644 g, 5 mmol) in
toluene (15 mL) and TMEDA (0.75 mL, 5 mmol) under nitrogen
at 278 °C. The mixture was then allowed to warm to room temper-
ature with constant stirring to give an orange solution. This solu-
tion was filtered under nitrogen and left at room temperature for
two days to yield a batch of dark-orange, needle-shaped crystals
(0.80 g, 52%). 2 M.p. 1442148 °C. 2 1H NMR (400 MHz, C6D6):
δ 5 2.59 (t, 2 H, -CH2Te-), 2.25 (s, 12 H, TMEDA), 2.05 (s, 4 H,
TMEDA), 1.92 (m, 2 H, -CH2-), 1.59 (m, 2 H, -CH2-), 1.03 (t, 3
H, CH3CH2-).
X-ray Analysis: Crystals of 1 and 2 were mounted directly into the
cold stream of an Oxford Crysotream crystal cooling apparatus,
installed on either a Stoe2Siemens four-circle diffractometer (1) or
a Siemens SMART CCD diffractometer (2), using perfluoropoly-
ether oil.
Crystal data for 1: C20H50Li2N4Se2, M 5 518.44, monoclinic, space
group I2/a, a 5 16.736(4), b 5 19.744(5), c 5 17.730(4) A˚ , β 5

91.13(3)°, U 5 5857(2) A˚ 3, Z 5 8, Dc 5 1.176 g cm23, µ(Mo-Kα) 5
2.535 mm21, T 5 180(2) K. 5916 Reflections (5145 unique, Rint 5
0.064) measured. Refinement on F2 values of all data gave final
wR2 5 0.2195, conventional R 5 0.0795 on F values of all
reflections with F2 . 2σ(F2), 292 parameters.
Crystal data for 2: C20H50Li2N4Te2, M 5 615.72, monoclinic, space
group I2/a, a 5 17.762(1), b 5 19.811(2), c 5 17.339(1) A˚ , β 5
90.77(1) °, U 5 6101.0(8) A˚ 3, Z 5 8, Dc 5 1.341 g cm23, µ(Mo-
Kα) 5 1.923 mm21, T 5 160(2) K. 27244 Reflections (7512 unique,
Rint 5 0.036) measured. Refinement on F2 values of all data gave
final wR2 5 0.1082, conventional R 5 0.0375 on F values of all
reflections with F2 . 2σ(F2), 291 parameters.
Crystallographic data for compounds 1 and 2 have been deposited
with the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre as supplemen-
tary publication nos. CCDC-151235 (1) and CCDC-152141 (2).
Copies of the data can be obtained free of charge on application
to CCDC, 12 Union Road, Cambridge, CB2 1EZ, UK [Fax
(internat.): 144-1223/336-033; Email: deposit@ccdc.cam.ac.uk;
www: http://www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk].
